text: This is one picture from our patients.

Throgoscopically, I think experienced throgoscopic surgeons can also do it.

only from the left side possible to make a radical thymectomy for these patients, young patients.

you just push, you just make very softly the tissue away from the phrenic nerve, and then you can just move it up in order not to cut anything, and no energy device.

The reason I wanted to approach this from the right side, usually if I have a very large lesion, I'll approach it from the ipsilateral side because I think it's harder to see where the phrenic is and things in relationship to it from the contralateral side.
But I knew from the beginning I could work anterior to the mass.
It was just sitting down in the chest.
And I committed early that I would probably go transmidestinal and open the other pleura.
And the reason is because some people say, well, you lose your space because of the, you know.
But mainly what I wanted to be able to do is after I mobilized it a little bit was, you'll see after I mobilized it is, push it into the other chest.
Because once I can push it into the other chest and it makes room on that side and then you can work.
I'm very careful to be very gentle visually and push it around, because you have to pay attention.
If you don't, you're going to break the capsule to mass, right?
Because you can only screw up this operation.
Because if you did it transternal, it'd be fine, no problem.
And it's a young, healthy person.
So I just tried to mobilize the fat, the thymus gland here enough so that I could then just very gently push this over to the other side.

You can see it's really vascular, and it turns out that it wound up having two separate, like a vein and an artery that were not your normal distribution for thymus gland, like wasn't a normal thymic vein.
So I'm just now working.
You can see there's much more room now.
I can work on this side.
I don't prefer to do a thymectomy generally from the right side, unless there's a right side of mass.

vein here, so, and I usually don't clip it.

So here's the innominate vein here.
I do think from the right side, as was already said, that it's much easier to find the innominate vein.
Then I used the harmonic to take the veins.
And now the mass is over here, and it's bleeding a little bit because I started to take some of the vessels.
So what I had to do was bring it back because it was somewhat limited.
So I took a big retractor that was gentle and I just brought it back.
Because you can't grab the capsule, right?
Because it's going to tear.
So I just used a big instrument to bring it back to the right side.
Here's the right lung here.
And then, you can't see it, but I used a 30 degree scope.
Once I crossed the mediastina, I wasn't that worried about
So he also had this artery here which, you know, normally is not that vigorous here.
No big deal, I just held onto it and I clipped it.
I had already taken the superior horns up here with the harmonic, easy to do that.
And then that was really just the last bit there.
And so I had visualized a phrenic on that side.
For thymic lesion without myasthenia, I don't get crazy about getting the fat all the way to the phrenic nerve.
But I do try to get phrenic to phrenic in a reasonable fashion.
And then the challenge was how to get the stupid thing out.
Like Serf was saying, I like the anterior inframammary incision because it's the widest and it's the easiest, so I put the bag in.
And this anchor bag, you have to be a little careful.
There are some sharp prongs there.
We had a case, one of my other partners, where, non-robotic, where they put this bag in and then it, like, stabbed the innominate vein and caused bleeding and they had to do a sternotomy and stuff.
So you've got to be careful about that.
And then, again, pushing it into the bag is always challenging.
And I have the fellow doing it, and you've got to watch the fellows because they don't pay enough visual attention.
Do you worry about pressure effects?
I don't really worry about it because I have visual evidence that I did a complete resection, so I don't care what the pathologists tell me.
No, I'm serious.
I mean, you know pathologists.
Are there any pathologists in the room?
So yeah, when you pull it out, the capsule gets ruptured in a spot, and that doesn't bother me because I can see that I did a complete resection.
Because there's no other way you're going to get it out.
And then I just put a single chest tube in the right chest.
It was a little bit bloodier than usual, but I don't put another incision to look at the left side.
I don't put another incision for a suction irrigator typically.
I just inflate the lung and...
And usually they go home in a day.
So I think robot is really great for that.

like a suboptimal biopsy.

the phrenic nerve here.

And even if you have a locally advanced fibroma where you have to resect some lung, et cetera, it's easy to do that.
I used...
I did use CO2 in her because I wanted to get the lung down as much as possible.
I usually don't use CO2 for most of my – if it's a posterior mediastinal case or this kind of somewhere in between middle mediastinal and anterior mediastinal.
Certainly, anterior mediastinal, I'll use CO2 in sufflation.
But here –
I wasn't sure I could get a complete resection, and I didn't want to do another sternotomy.

I don't think it really matters what energy source you use.

If you want to use monopolar scissors, that's fine.
If you like the hook, that's fine.
Whatever you're good at.
From a cancer standpoint, there's probably a marginally...
you know, useful procedure because I'm not going to get an R0 resection in her, but I was trying to get at least an R1 resection and then give her some radiation post-op.
She had never seen radiation.

I don't use it for a thymectomy, routine thymectomy.
It's unnecessary.

I like the bipolar in my left hand, you see that there?
So if there's any bleeding, I just cauterize it.
But I certainly think that using the Maryland or the bipolar thoracic dissector is good.
It just requires a little bit more training.
I haven't had too many problems with the monopolar cautery.
I typically do very, this was now tissue that was completely behind this keba.
I got just used to, after thousands of cases, a very pinpoint cautery.
I don't get into many problems.
And then I really try to use my vision to see where the plane is.
But I do think bipolar is very safe.
It was more natural for me to use the spatula because it was like a bovie.
It was easier for me rather than learning how to bipolar and tear.
So I think this saved this lady a redo sternotomy that she didn't need.
So I think it's a nice application of the robot.
You could argue with me that it was kind of a marginally indicated case oncologically for thymic carcinoma.
She did well and went home in a day, less than a day, and then she got chemo and RT.
